claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for UK Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.**

**How old is the child?**

Please let me know which category applies:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, please tell me about each of them and their ages.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to check if I can claim Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who's 17 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you! Let me check the eligibility requirements for Alex, who is 17 years old.

Since Alex is between 16 and 19, I need to check if they meet certain education or training requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For children aged 16-19 like Alex, Child Benefit can continue if they meet certain education or training criteria.

**Is Alex currently:**

1. **In approved education or training** (such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQs up to level 3, an apprenticeship, or similar qualifications)?

2. **Recently left education or training** (within the last few months)?

3. **Neither** - not in education/training and hasn't recently left?

Please let me know which situation applies to Alex.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is still in education - he's doing his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! Alex is in education doing A-levels. Let me verify that this meets the approved education criteria.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! A-levels are on the approved list of qualifications. I just need to confirm a couple more details about Alex's studies:

**A-levels at sixth form college are approved education.** To confirm eligibility, I need to check:

1. **Is Alex studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study/course time)

2. **Is the course funded by an employer or linked to a job offer from an employer?** (Employer-funded training would not qualify)

For most sixth form A-level students, the answer would typically be "yes" to the first question and "no" to the second - but please confirm this is the case for Alex.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's studying full-time at the sixth form college - it's a regular A-level course, not anything linked to an employer.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's great - Alex's A-level course qualifies as approved education. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Now I need to check Alex's work status.

**Does Alex work less than 24 hours per week** (or is he not working at all)?

This includes any paid employment alongside his studies.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, he does work - he has a part-time job at the local supermarket. It's quite a lot of hours, probably around 24 hours a week or maybe a bit more. He works evenings and weekends to save up some money.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know. This is an important detail.

The rules state that children aged 16 or over who work **24 hours or more per week** are not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they're in approved education.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see - the maximum hours allowed is **24 hours per week**. Since Alex is working around 24 hours or possibly more, this is right on the threshold.

**This is important:** If Alex is working **24 hours or more per week**, you would not be eligible for Child Benefit for him, even though he's doing his A-levels.

Could you clarify - do you know more precisely how many hours Alex typically works? 

- **If it's under 24 hours per week** (e.g., 23 hours or less), you would still be eligible
- **If it's 24 hours or more per week**, unfortunately this would make him ineligible

It might be worth checking his work contract or recent payslips to see his exact hours, as this makes a significant difference to eligibility.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Let me think... he usually does three evenings after college during the week, and then most of Saturday and Sunday. I'd say it's definitely at least 24 hours, probably a bit more when you add it all up. He's quite committed to the job.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for clarifying. Unfortunately, since Alex is working 24 hours or more per week, this affects his eligibility for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I've now reached an outcome for Alex. Let me provide you with the final assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Result

**Result: NOT ELIGIBLE** ❌

### Summary for Alex (age 17)

Unfortunately, you are **not currently eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

**Why?**
While Alex meets several criteria (he's under 20 and in approved full-time education doing A-levels), the rules state that children aged 16 or over who work **24 or more hours per week** are not eligible for Child Benefit - even if they're still in education.

Since Alex works approximately 24+ hours per week at the supermarket, this makes him ineligible.

---

### What you could do

There are a couple of options to consider:

1. **Reduce Alex's working hours** - If Alex reduced his hours to **under 24 per week**, you could become eligible for Child Benefit again. Even a small reduction (e.g., to 23 hours) would make a difference.

2. **Keep track of changes** - If Alex's circumstances change in the future (e.g., he cuts back on work during exam periods), you may be able to claim at that point.

3. **Check again when circumstances change** - If Alex finishes his job or significantly reduces his hours while still in approved education, you can make a new claim.

---

I understand this may be disappointing news, especially since Alex is clearly working hard both at college and in his job. If you have any questions or if Alex's situation changes, please don't hesitate to check eligibility again.

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and is in approved education (full-time A-levels at sixth form college), which would normally qualify for Child Benefit. However, Alex also works part-time at a local supermarket for approximately 24 hours or more per week. Under Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24 or more hours per week are not eligible, even if they are also in approved education. Since Alex's work hours meet or exceed this 24-hour threshold, he does not qualify for Child Benefit.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 17), who is studying A-levels full-time but also working 24+ hours per week. The work hours exceed the permitted limit for Child Benefit eligibility for children aged 16+, making the family ineligible for Child Benefit at this time.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.reason: Child aged 16+ is working 24 or more hours per week and not in approved education or training
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops if child aged 16 or over starts paid work for 24+ hours per week and is no longer in approved education or training. If they return to education/training, you may be able to claim again.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and is in approved education (full-time A-levels at sixth form college), which would normally qualify for Child Benefit. However, Alex also works part-time at a local supermarket for approximately 24 hours or more per week. Under Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24 or more hours per week are not eligible, even if they are also in approved education. Since Alex's work hours meet or exceed this 24-hour threshold, he does not qualify for Child Benefit.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (age 17), who is studying A-levels full-time but also working 24+ hours per week. The work hours exceed the permitted limit for Child Benefit eligibility for children aged 16+, making the family ineligible for Child Benefit at this time.
